name: Strasbourg Christkindelsmarik
description: The oldest and most celebrated Christmas market in France, running since 1570 — and by most accounts the one that defined what a Christmas market should be. Eleven sites across the city, anchored at Place Broglie and Place de la Cathédrale beneath the pink sandstone spire. The mulled wine (vin chaud), foie gras, and bredele biscuits are the standard. Strasbourg calls itself the Capital of Christmas and earns it.

name: Nuremberg Christkindlesmarkt
description: Germany's most famous Christmas market, held on the medieval Hauptmarkt beneath the Church of Our Lady since 1628. The Christkind — a golden-haired angel — opens the market with a proclamation every year. Famous for Lebkuchen (gingerbread), Zwetschgenmännle (prune figures), and Nuremberg Rostbratwurst. Two million visitors annually. Arrive early in the day.

name: Vienna Wiener Christkindlmarkt
description: The grandest Christmas market setting in Europe — 150 stalls spread across the illuminated Rathausplatz facing Vienna's neo-Gothic City Hall. Running since 1975 but rooted in a tradition dating to the 13th century. Austrian handicrafts, punch, roasted chestnuts, and classical music concerts on the outdoor stage. The skating rink in the park adds to the atmosphere.

name: Cologne Cathedral Christmas Market
description: Six distinct Christmas markets across the city, the most dramatic being the one at Roncalliplatz directly beneath the twin spires of Cologne Cathedral — a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The cathedral lit at night with the market below is one of the great Christmas images in Europe. The mulled wine comes in collectible mugs shaped like the cathedral.

name: Prague Old Town Square Christmas Market
description: One of the most visually spectacular Christmas markets in Europe, set in the medieval Old Town Square beneath the Astronomical Clock and the Church of Our Lady before Týn. A giant decorated Christmas tree, wooden stalls selling trdelník (chimney cake), svařák (mulled wine), and Czech crafts. The setting is unmatched — it looks like a film set.

name: Budapest Christmas Market
description: Vörösmarty Square transforms into one of Central Europe's most refined Christmas markets, with Hungarian craft stalls, kürtőskalács (chimney cake), palinka, and exceptional food from local producers. A second market at St. Stephen's Basilica — lit dramatically at night — is smaller and more atmospheric. Repeatedly voted among the best markets in Europe.

name: Brussels Winter Wonders
description: Belgium's largest Christmas event spreads from the Grand Place — one of the world's most beautiful squares — through the historic center. A Ferris wheel, ice rink, light show on the Grand Place, and 200+ stalls selling Belgian waffles, hot chocolate, and speculaas. The nightly light-and-sound show on the Gothic guild houses is free and unmissable.

name: Edinburgh Christmas Market
description: Scotland's Christmas market stretches along Princes Street Gardens below the floodlit Edinburgh Castle — one of the most dramatic backdrops of any market in the world. German-style stalls, a big wheel, ice rink, and Scottish food and craft producers. The view up to the castle from the market at night is exceptional.
address: Princes Street Gardens, Edinburgh EH2, Scotland

name: Tivoli Gardens Christmas
description: The world's second-oldest amusement park — open since 1843 — becomes one of Europe's most magical Christmas destinations. Half a million lights, a traditional Danish market, rides running in the cold, and glögg (mulled wine) at every turn. The Victorian gardens covered in snow and lights are unlike any Christmas market experience elsewhere.

name: Munich Christkindlmarkt
description: Munich's main Christmas market at Marienplatz — beneath the famous Glockenspiel carillon — has run continuously since 1642. Traditional Bavarian food and crafts, with strong competition from the nearby Krampus market, medieval market, and Schwabing market. The Nativity market near the Frauenkirche is particularly beautiful.

name: Dresden Striezelmarkt
description: Germany's oldest Christmas market, documented since 1434, held on the Altmarkt in the heart of Dresden. Famous for the Dresdner Stollen — the rich fruit bread sold nowhere better than here — and for the enormous pyramid and nutcracker figures. The market's name comes from Striezel, an old German word for the Stollen loaf. Five centuries of tradition.

name: Bruges Christmas Market
description: The most picturesque Christmas market in Belgium — possibly in all of Europe. Stalls lining the Markt and Simon Stevinplein in a medieval city that was built for this aesthetic. Ice skating on the Minnewater lake, Belgian chocolate, hot gluhwein, and the Belfry of Bruges lit at night. The entire city feels like a Christmas card.

name: Heidelberg Christmas Market
description: One of Germany's most romantically sited markets, spread across five locations in Heidelberg's beautifully preserved old town, with the castle illuminated above. The market on the Marktplatz around the Church of the Holy Spirit is the oldest. Glühwein, gingerbread, and the half-timbered townscape make this feel more authentically German than the tourist-heavy mega-markets.

name: Zurich Christmas Markets
description: Zurich hosts two unmissable markets: the outdoor market at the Hauptbahnhof — Europe's largest indoor Christmas market inside the grand Victorian train station, with a tree hung with 7,000 Swarovski crystals — and the traditional market on Werdmühleplatz. Swiss precision applied to Christmas.

name: Tallinn Christmas Market
description: The medieval Town Hall Square in Tallinn's UNESCO-listed old town hosts one of the most atmospheric Christmas markets in Northern Europe. Estonia claims to have invented the Christmas tree tradition — a tree has stood in the square since 1441. Mulled wine, roasted almonds, and traditional Estonian handicrafts under medieval limestone towers.

name: Riga Christmas Market
description: Riga's Dome Square hosts a Christmas market with a strong claim to historical importance — Latvia credits Riga with erecting the world's first decorated Christmas tree in 1510. The market in front of the Riga Cathedral is intimate, well-curated, and surrounded by some of the finest Art Nouveau architecture in Europe. Excellent Latvian craft stalls.

name: Rothenburg ob der Tauber Reiterlesmarkt
description: Germany's most perfectly preserved medieval walled town becomes the country's most fairytale Christmas destination. Snow on the half-timbered houses, the Reiterlesmarkt in the market square, and the Käthe Wohlfahrt Christmas Village — the world's largest permanent Christmas shop — make this unlike any other market. Smaller and more authentic than the big-city markets.
address: Marktplatz, 91541 Rothenburg ob der Tauber, Germany

name: London Winter Wonderland
description: The UK's largest Christmas event in Hyde Park — ice rinks, a Bavarian Village with 100+ market stalls, a circus, ice sculptures, fairground rides, and a giant observation wheel. More funfair than traditional market, but enormous fun and centrally located. The Giant Observation Wheel view over London at night is genuinely spectacular. Book in advance; it gets very crowded.
address: Hyde Park, London W2 2UH, United Kingdom

name: Paris Champs-Élysées Christmas Market
description: Two kilometres of wooden chalets lining the world's most famous avenue, from Place de la Concorde to the Arc de Triomphe. The Champs-Élysées lit with thousands of lights forming a canopy overhead is one of the great Christmas spectacles in Europe. French regional products, vin chaud, and the Ferris wheel at Concorde complete the picture.

name: Innsbruck Christmas Markets
description: Five Christmas markets scattered through Austria's Alpine capital, with the Nordkette mountain range as backdrop. The Old Town market beneath the Golden Roof is the most beautiful — a compact, high-quality market with Austrian handicrafts, Tyrolean schnapps, and hot punch. The combination of Baroque architecture, Alpine peaks, and Christmas lights is unique in Europe.
